---
title: "SAR"
---

A key point about SAR is that it is a radar (RAdio Detection And Ranging) instrument, and its basic measurements are intensity (or amplitude) and phase of the backscattered signal, sampled in time bins along the azimuth (along track direction of the sensor antenna) and range (across track or perpendicular to the direction of the sensor antenna). 

image: Synthetic Aperture Radar "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\SLAR-Imagery-678x479.jpg" [https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/s3fs-public/2024-06/sci-sar-userguide.pdf]


## Summary & Application


###  How does the **"active"** nature and **wavelength** of SAR sensors differentiate their operational capacity from passive optical sensors?


::: column-page
```{r, echo=FALSE}
xaringanExtra::embed_xaringan(
  url = "https://jiaying-ice.github.io/Jiaying-2026-RS-Diary/index.html#7",
)
```
:::

Unlike passive optical sensors that rely on solar reflection and are obstructed by cloud cover or darkness, SAR is an active sensor that emits its own microwave signals
. The wavelength (e.g., C-band at ~5.5 cm) allows these signals to pass through clouds, smoke, volcanic ash, and even dense atmospheric conditions
.
Application: This capacity makes SAR a "game changer" for disaster response
. For example, when optical sensors fail during extreme weather or nighttime, C-band SAR can reliably monitor sea ice, oil spills, and flooded areas
. The penetration depth also varies with wavelength; while X-band scatters at the surface, C and L-bands can penetrate deeper into vegetation canopies to assess biomass or soil moisture
.
[]

###  What are the physical distinctions between **signal amplitude and phase**, and why is phase information critical for **InSAR** applications?

A SAR signal consists of two primary data components: Amplitude (backscatter), which measures the strength of the radar response and reflects surface properties like roughness and moisture; and Phase, which identifies the specific location of the wave on its cycle when it returns to the sensor. 


image:surface roughness on the sensor wavelength λ: (a) smooth, (b) intermediate, and (c) rough. 
https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/CHAPTER-2-Spaceborne-Synthetic-Aperture-Radar-%3A-%2C-%2C-Meyer/1827004c921f186c06d536deb81a7f9b3c12b623 


While Google Earth Engine primarily utilizes amplitude for visualization and basic change detection, the phase is essential for Interferometry (InSAR). 


InSAR exploits the phase difference between two complex observations of the same area to extract distance information, enabling the creation of Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) and the monitoring of millimeter-scale ground deformations like land subsidence or volcanic uplift




###  How do different scattering mechanisms (Double-bounce, Volume, and Rough Surface) correlate with specific SAR polarizations (HH, VV, VH)?

Image：synthetic aperture radar  "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\SAR.png" https://gisgeography.com/passive-active-sensors-remote-sensing/


For the untrained eye, it’s just a bunch of black and white pixels. But the reality is that there’s more than meets the eye. For example, the 3 main types of backscatter are: Specular reflection; Double-bounce; Diffuse scattering.



image:three main scattering types considered for SAR data. 
"D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\scattering_types.png"
https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/CHAPTER-2-Spaceborne-Synthetic-Aperture-Radar-%3A-%2C-%2C-Meyer/1827004c921f186c06d536deb81a7f9b3c12b623

The way radar waves interact with the Earth's surface depends on the target's geometry and the signal's polarization (the orientation of the wave's oscillation).  

- Rough Surface Scattering: Best detected via VV polarization; it occurs on surfaces like bare earth or water where waves scatter in various directions  

- Double-bounce Scattering: Best detected via HH polarization; this is typical of man-made structures (buildings) or tree trunks where the signal reflects twice before returning  

- Volume Scattering: Best detected via cross-polarization (VH or HV); this occurs when signals bounce multiple times within complex structures like forest canopies  

imgae:SAR signal penetration by sensor wavelength λ. ["D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\6-Figure2.6-1.png"] 
https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/CHAPTER-2-Spaceborne-Synthetic-Aperture-Radar-%3A-%2C-%2C-Meyer/1827004c921f186c06d536deb81a7f9b3c12b623 


This following image, derived from research on the Sentinel-1 Global Backscattering Model, showcases C-band microwave observations from the Bordeaux region of France. By comparing different polarization schemes and processing methods, the image visually reveals the relationship between surface physical properties and radar signals.

image: Detail of S1GBM global VH and W mosaics, compared against land cover and non-normalised backscatter "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\vvvhpng.png" [@bauer-marschallinger2021]

This responds to scattering mechanisms by demonstrating that VH polarization is most sensitive to Volume Scattering, providing a clear discrimination of forest density and agricultural structures due to multiple scattering within canopies, whereas VV polarization excels at capturing Rough Surface Scattering, which yields high spatial detail for urban infrastructure.

###  What are the primary geometric and radiometric distortions inherent in SAR data, and how are they mitigated?


Due to SAR’s side-looking (oblique) observation geometry, images suffer from distortions like foreshortening (slopes appearing shorter), layover (tops of mountains imaged ahead of their base), and shadowing.  


Additionally, SAR images often exhibit "Speckle," a grainy salt-and-pepper noise caused by constructive and destructive interference of coherent signals within a single pixel.  

These are mitigated through Radiometric Terrain Correction (RTC), which uses a DEM to normalize backscatter values based on the local incidence angle, and Speckle filtering (e.g., Refined Lee filter) to smooth the image while preserving edges.  

image: Main geometric distortions on SAR images with their dependence on acquisition geometry: (a) foreshortening, (b) layover, and (c) shadow. ["D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\4-Figure2.4-1.png"] 
https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/CHAPTER-2-Spaceborne-Synthetic-Aperture-Radar-%3A-%2C-%2C-Meyer/1827004c921f186c06d536deb81a7f9b3c12b623 



###  In what ways can SAR data be utilized for advanced change detection and urban policy monitoring compared to traditional "image subtraction"?


Simple image subtraction is often insufficient for SAR due to its statistical properties; instead, researchers use ratio or log-ratio methods to identify change.   

Advanced monitoring uses the high temporal revisit of satellites like Sentinel-1 (every 6–12 days) to analyze variance over time through t-tests or standard deviation. 
  
Practically, SAR is necessary for urban policy because texture measures can discern urban forms (high-density vs. residential) even in cloudy regions, and its ability to map low backscatter regions (which appear dark) makes it the gold standard for mapping the extent of floods regardless of weather conditions.  






## Reflection

Having explored the Sentinel-1 mission in Week 2, returning to **SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar)** this week felt like visiting an old friend with a new/high level of depth. My basic knowledge of SAR as an active sensor who capable of seeing through clouds and darkness was put into practical.   

A takeaway this week was the distinction between **Amplitude** and **Phase**. While Google Earth Engine primarily utilizes amplitude for visualization and basic change detection, I’ve realized that phase is the only determined essential for **Interferometry (InSAR)**. This allows us to move beyond 2D mapping into the realm of 3D displacement and surface deformation, which optical sensors simply cannot do!!  

My understanding of radar isn't just theoretical; it is currently being stress-tested in our ongoing group project. We are integrating radar technology to detect the PV panels on the Rooftop of Barcelona.

> **Project Integration:** You can view our current progress and how we are embedding radar insights into our workflow here:
> [View our Xaringan Presentation](https://jiaying-ice.github.io/Jiaying-2026-RS-Diary/index.html)

I'm confused the difference between general **Radar** and **SAR** and now it's time to clarify:  

* **Radar (Radio Detection and Ranging)**is a broad technology that sends out radio waves to detect the range, angle, or velocity of objects.
* **SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar)** is a specific, sophisticated form of radar. And for Synthetic, by utilizing the motion of the satellite antenna over a target area, SAR "synthesizes" a much larger antenna (aperture), allowing for high-resolution images that standard radar systems could never achieve from space.  

With the formal learning and practicals concluded, I find myself in a state of opening myself to every characters in life. I am no longer just a data analyst, I could be a consultant, could be a official staff who work for the goverments. BUt all in one, I am dedicated to improving the urban environment through the analysis and prediction of multi-source data.  

Now, I understand that every policy recommendation—whether it is a solar plan for NYC or a sustainability audit for Barcelona all of them must be backed by a transparent, reproducible, and validated remote sensing workflow.  

BYE, Hope you enjoy my Learning diary.
